instruction: How do I use tokio-console and tracing to debug async Rust services in production, including task inspection and resource tracking?
output:
<think>
tokio-console is a diagnostic tool for async Rust apps that provides real-time visibility into Tokio tasks, resources, and wakers — similar to htop but for async tasks. It works by subscribing to tracing events via the console-subscriber crate. In production, you need careful setup: (1) the subscriber adds overhead (~5-10%), so use feature flags or runtime toggles, (2) the console gRPC server should only bind to localhost, (3) combine with tracing-subscriber layers for structured logging. Key diagnostic capabilities: see which tasks are running/idle/blocked, track task poll times (finding slow polls that block the executor), monitor resource contention (mutexes, semaphores), and identify task spawning patterns. The tracing ecosystem layers: console-subscriber for tokio-console, tracing-flame for flame graphs, tracing-opentelemetry for distributed tracing. I should show the layered subscriber setup and how to correlate task IDs with business logic.
</think>

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[dependencies]
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full", "tracing"] }
tracing = "0.1"
tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter", "json", "fmt"] }
console-subscriber = { version = "0.3", optional = true }
tracing-flame = { version = "0.2", optional = true }
tracing-opentelemetry = { version = "0.23", optional = true }
opentelemetry = { version = "0.22", optional = true }
opentelemetry-otlp = { version = "0.15", optional = true }

[features]
default = []
console = ["dep:console-subscriber"]
flame = ["dep:tracing-flame"]
otel = ["dep:tracing-opentelemetry", "dep:opentelemetry", "dep:opentelemetry-otlp"]
```

```rust
// src/telemetry.rs — Layered tracing setup for production
use tracing_subscriber::{fmt, prelude::*, EnvFilter, Registry};

pub fn init_telemetry() {
    let env_filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()
        .unwrap_or_else(|_| EnvFilter::new("info,my_service=debug,tower=warn"));

    let fmt_layer = fmt::layer()
        .json()
        .with_target(true)
        .with_thread_ids(true)
        .with_span_events(fmt::format::FmtSpan::CLOSE);

    let registry = Registry::default()
        .with(env_filter)
        .with(fmt_layer);

    // Conditionally add tokio-console layer
    #[cfg(feature = "console")]
    let registry = {
        let console_layer = console_subscriber::ConsoleLayer::builder()
            .server_addr(([127, 0, 0, 1], 6669))  // localhost only!
            .retention(std::time::Duration::from_secs(60))
            .spawn();
        registry.with(console_layer)
    };

    // Conditionally add flame graph layer
    #[cfg(feature = "flame")]
    let registry = {
        let (flame_layer, _guard) = tracing_flame::FlameLayer::with_file("./tracing.folded")
            .unwrap();
        // Store guard in a static to keep writer alive
        std::mem::forget(_guard);
        registry.with(flame_layer)
    };

    registry.init();
}
```

```rust
// src/main.rs — Application with task instrumentation
use std::time::Duration;
use tokio::sync::Semaphore;
use tracing::{info, warn, instrument, Instrument};

mod telemetry;

static DB_SEMAPHORE: Semaphore = Semaphore::const_new(50);

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    telemetry::init_telemetry();
    info!("Service starting");

    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080").await.unwrap();

    loop {
        let (stream, addr) = listener.accept().await.unwrap();

        // Named task — visible in tokio-console
        tokio::task::Builder::new()
            .name(&format!("conn-{addr}"))
            .spawn(handle_connection(stream, addr).in_current_span())
            .unwrap();
    }
}

#[instrument(skip(stream), fields(client = %addr))]
async fn handle_connection(
    stream: tokio::net::TcpStream,
    addr: std::net::SocketAddr,
) {
    info!("New connection");

    // Acquire database connection permit — visible as resource in console
    let _permit = DB_SEMAPHORE.acquire().await.unwrap();

    let result = process_request(&stream).await;
    match result {
        Ok(()) => info!("Request completed"),
        Err(e) => warn!(error = %e, "Request failed"),
    }
}

#[instrument(skip(stream))]
async fn process_request(
    stream: &tokio::net::TcpStream,
) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    // Simulate work — poll duration visible in tokio-console
    let data = query_database().await?;
    let result = tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || {
        // CPU-bound work — shows as blocking task in console
        expensive_computation(&data)
    }).await??;

    Ok(())
}

#[instrument]
async fn query_database() -> Result<Vec<u8>, Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    // Simulated DB query
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(50)).await;
    Ok(vec![1, 2, 3])
}

fn expensive_computation(data: &[u8]) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(100));
    Ok(())
}
```

```bash
# Run with tokio-console enabled
cargo run --features console

# Connect tokio-console from another terminal (or remote via SSH tunnel)
tokio-console http://localhost:6669

# Shows:
# - All spawned tasks with names, poll counts, busy/idle time
# - Resources (Semaphores, Mutexes) with waiters
# - Waker statistics
# - Task state transitions

# Generate flame graph
cargo run --features flame
# After stopping, convert:
inferno-flamegraph tracing.folded > flamegraph.svg
```

instruction: Show how to analyze a Rust core dump from a production crash, including extracting backtraces and inspecting async task state with gdb.
output:
<think>
Rust core dump analysis is harder than C because of name mangling, optimized builds stripping debug info, and async state machines being compiled into enum variants. The workflow: (1) ensure the binary has debug info (even release builds should have `debug = 1` or split debuginfo), (2) configure core dumps on the system, (3) load the core in gdb, (4) use `rust-gdb` wrapper which loads pretty-printers. For async code, each .await point compiles into a state machine variant — you can inspect the enum to see which .await the task was suspended at. Key: release builds with debug = 1 (DWARF info) add ~20% binary size but make crashes debuggable. The `strip = "debuginfo"` in Cargo.toml can be paired with split debuginfo so the main binary stays small but .dwo/.dwp files exist for debugging. I should also show addr2line for quick backtrace symbolication without gdb.
</think>

```toml
# Cargo.toml — Release profile with debug info
[profile.release]
opt-level = 3
debug = 1                    # DWARF debug info (adds ~20% size)
split-debuginfo = "packed"   # .dwp file separate from binary
panic = "abort"              # Cleaner core dumps (no unwinding)
strip = false                # Don't strip — we want symbols

[profile.release-prod]
inherits = "release"
strip = "symbols"            # Strip binary, keep .dwp for debugging
split-debuginfo = "packed"   # Debug info in separate .dwp file
```

```rust
// src/crash_handler.rs — Enable core dumps and signal handling
use std::io::Write;

pub fn setup_crash_handler() {
    // Enable core dumps
    #[cfg(unix)]
    unsafe {
        libc::setrlimit(
            libc::RLIMIT_CORE,
            &libc::rlimit {
                rlim_cur: libc::RLIM_INFINITY,
                rlim_max: libc::RLIM_INFINITY,
            },
        );
    }

    // Custom panic hook that logs before abort
    std::panic::set_hook(Box::new(|info| {
        let backtrace = std::backtrace::Backtrace::force_capture();
        eprintln!("PANIC: {info}");
        eprintln!("Backtrace:\n{backtrace}");

        // Also write to crash log file
        if let Ok(mut f) = std::fs::File::create("/tmp/rust_panic.log") {
            let _ = writeln!(f, "PANIC: {info}");
            let _ = writeln!(f, "Backtrace:\n{backtrace}");
        }
    }));
}
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# scripts/analyze_rust_core.sh — Analyze Rust core dump
set -euo pipefail

BINARY="$1"
CORE="$2"

echo "=== Analyzing Rust core dump ==="
echo "Binary: $BINARY"
echo "Core: $CORE"

# Use rust-gdb for pretty-printing
rust-gdb -batch \
    -ex "set pagination off" \
    -ex "set print pretty on" \
    -ex "file $BINARY" \
    -ex "core-file $CORE" \
    -ex "echo \n=== Signal Info ===\n" \
    -ex "info signal" \
    -ex "echo \n=== Backtrace (all frames) ===\n" \
    -ex "bt full" \
    -ex "echo \n=== All Threads ===\n" \
    -ex "info threads" \
    -ex "echo \n=== All Thread Backtraces ===\n" \
    -ex "thread apply all bt" \
    -ex "echo \n=== Registers ===\n" \
    -ex "info registers" \
    -ex quit 2>&1 | tee /tmp/core_analysis.txt

echo ""
echo "=== Quick symbolication with addr2line ==="
# Extract addresses from backtrace and symbolicate
grep -oP '0x[0-9a-f]+' /tmp/core_analysis.txt | head -20 | while read addr; do
    result=$(addr2line -e "$BINARY" -f -p "$addr" 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$result" ] && ! echo "$result" | grep -q '??'; then
        echo "  $addr -> $result"
    fi
done
```

```bash
# Interactive GDB session for async state inspection
rust-gdb ./target/release/my-service core.12345

# In GDB:
(gdb) bt
# #0 tokio::runtime::scheduler::multi_thread::worker::Context::run
# #1 my_service::handle_connection::{{closure}}::{{closure}}

# Inspect the async state machine
(gdb) frame 1
(gdb) info locals
# __awaitee = GenFuture::Suspend1 { data: [...], db_result: Pending }
# This tells us: the task was suspended at the 2nd .await (Suspend1)
# and db_result was still Pending (waiting for DB response)

# Print the future state enum
(gdb) print *future
# Shows which variant of the generated state machine is active

# Find all tokio worker threads
(gdb) info threads
# Lists all threads — tokio workers are named "tokio-runtime-worker"

# Check what each worker was doing
(gdb) thread apply all bt 5
```

```rust
// Example: async state machine inspection
// This async function:
async fn process(db: &Pool, id: u64) -> Result<Response, Error> {
    let user = db.get_user(id).await?;     // Suspend point 0
    let perms = db.get_perms(id).await?;    // Suspend point 1
    let result = compute(user, perms).await; // Suspend point 2
    Ok(result)
}

// Compiles to something like:
// enum ProcessFuture {
//     Suspend0 { db: &Pool, id: u64, __awaitee: GetUserFuture },
//     Suspend1 { user: User, db: &Pool, id: u64, __awaitee: GetPermsFuture },
//     Suspend2 { user: User, perms: Perms, __awaitee: ComputeFuture },
//     Complete,
// }
// In GDB, the variant name tells you which .await the task was at.
```

instruction: How do I use perf and tracing-flame to generate flame graphs for a Rust async service to identify performance bottlenecks?
output:
<think>
Flame graphs for Rust async services need special handling because: (1) async code compiles to state machines with mangled names, (2) tokio runtime threads may show deep runtime frames that obscure application code, (3) you need both on-CPU (what's running) and off-CPU (what's waiting) analysis. Two approaches: tracing-flame (application-level, sees async span boundaries) and perf (system-level, sees actual CPU usage). tracing-flame generates folded stacks from tracing spans — it understands your application structure. perf captures actual CPU samples — it shows where CPU time goes including runtime overhead. The best practice is to use both: tracing-flame for logical async flow analysis, perf for actual CPU hotspots. For perf with Rust, you need debug symbols and the rust demangler. I should show both approaches and how to combine them.
</think>

```rust
// src/telemetry.rs — Flame graph setup with tracing-flame
use std::sync::Mutex;
use tracing_flame::FlameLayer;
use tracing_subscriber::{fmt, prelude::*, EnvFilter, Registry};

static FLAME_GUARD: Mutex<Option<tracing_flame::FlushGuard<std::io::BufWriter<std::fs::File>>>> =
    Mutex::new(None);

pub fn init_with_flame(flame_file: &str) {
    let env_filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()
        .unwrap_or_else(|_| EnvFilter::new("info,my_service=trace"));

    let fmt_layer = fmt::layer().with_target(true);

    let (flame_layer, guard) = FlameLayer::with_file(flame_file).unwrap();

    // Store the guard so it flushes on drop
    *FLAME_GUARD.lock().unwrap() = Some(guard);

    Registry::default()
        .with(env_filter)
        .with(fmt_layer)
        .with(flame_layer)
        .init();
}

pub fn flush_flame() {
    // Drop the guard to flush the flame data
    FLAME_GUARD.lock().unwrap().take();
}
```

```rust
// src/main.rs — Instrumented service
use std::time::Duration;
use tracing::instrument;

mod telemetry;

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    telemetry::init_with_flame("./tracing.folded");

    // Run the service for profiling period
    let server = tokio::spawn(run_server());

    // Profile for 30 seconds then flush
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(30)).await;
    telemetry::flush_flame();
    eprintln!("Flame data written to tracing.folded");

    server.abort();
}

#[instrument]
async fn run_server() {
    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080").await.unwrap();
    loop {
        let (stream, _) = listener.accept().await.unwrap();
        tokio::spawn(handle_request(stream));
    }
}

#[instrument(skip(stream))]
async fn handle_request(stream: tokio::net::TcpStream) {
    let data = read_request(&stream).await;
    let parsed = parse_payload(&data).await;
    let result = query_database(&parsed).await;
    serialize_response(&stream, &result).await;
}

#[instrument(skip(stream))]
async fn read_request(stream: &tokio::net::TcpStream) -> Vec<u8> {
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_micros(100)).await;
    vec![1, 2, 3]
}

#[instrument]
async fn parse_payload(data: &[u8]) -> String {
    // CPU-bound work — shows up in perf flame graph
    let mut result = String::new();
    for &b in data {
        result.push_str(&format!("{:02x}", b));
    }
    result
}

#[instrument]
async fn query_database(query: &str) -> Vec<u8> {
    // I/O wait — shows up in tracing-flame but not perf on-CPU
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(5)).await;
    vec![4, 5, 6]
}

#[instrument(skip(stream))]
async fn serialize_response(stream: &tokio::net::TcpStream, data: &[u8]) {
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_micros(50)).await;
}
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# scripts/flame_profile.sh — Complete flame graph workflow
set -euo pipefail

BINARY="./target/release/my-service"
DURATION=30

echo "=== Method 1: tracing-flame (async-aware) ==="
cargo build --release --features flame
$BINARY &
SERVICE_PID=$!
sleep 2

# Generate load
wrk -t4 -c100 -d${DURATION}s http://localhost:8080/

wait $SERVICE_PID 2>/dev/null || true

# Convert folded stacks to SVG flame graph
inferno-flamegraph tracing.folded > flame_tracing.svg
echo "Async flame graph: flame_tracing.svg"


echo "=== Method 2: perf (CPU-level) ==="
cargo build --release  # debug=1 in release profile
$BINARY &
SERVICE_PID=$!
sleep 2

# Record CPU samples
perf record -F 99 -g -p $SERVICE_PID -- sleep $DURATION &
PERF_PID=$!

# Generate load during perf recording
wrk -t4 -c100 -d${DURATION}s http://localhost:8080/

wait $PERF_PID
kill $SERVICE_PID 2>/dev/null || true

# Convert perf data to flame graph
perf script | inferno-collapse-perf | rustfilt | inferno-flamegraph > flame_perf.svg
echo "CPU flame graph: flame_perf.svg"


echo "=== Method 3: perf with async task names ==="
# Use perf with DWARF unwinding for better async stacks
perf record -F 99 --call-graph dwarf -p $SERVICE_PID -- sleep $DURATION
perf script | inferno-collapse-perf --all | rustfilt | inferno-flamegraph \
    --title "Async Service CPU Profile" \
    --subtitle "$(date)" \
    > flame_perf_dwarf.svg
```

instruction: How do I set up structured logging with tracing in a Rust microservice for production, including correlation IDs, error context, and log sampling?
output:
<think>
Structured logging in Rust production services needs several layers: (1) tracing for instrumentation (spans + events), (2) tracing-subscriber for output formatting, (3) correlation ID propagation across async boundaries, (4) error context enrichment, (5) log sampling to reduce volume without losing important events. The tracing ecosystem is composable via layers — you can stack JSON formatting, OpenTelemetry export, and console output. For correlation IDs, the pattern is: extract from incoming request header (or generate), store in a tracing span, and it automatically propagates to all child spans/events. Log sampling should be done carefully: always log errors and warnings, sample INFO at a configurable rate, and sample DEBUG/TRACE heavily. The EnvFilter handles per-target log levels, but for sampling we need a custom layer.
</think>

```rust
// src/telemetry.rs — Production structured logging
use std::time::Duration;
use tracing::{Subscriber, Level};
use tracing_subscriber::{
    fmt::{self, format::JsonFields},
    layer::SubscriberExt,
    util::SubscriberInitExt,
    EnvFilter, Layer, Registry,
};

pub fn init_production_logging() {
    let env_filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env().unwrap_or_else(|_| {
        EnvFilter::new(
            "info,my_service=debug,tower_http=debug,sqlx=warn,hyper=warn"
        )
    });

    // JSON layer for log aggregation (stdout → container log driver)
    let json_layer = fmt::layer()
        .json()
        .with_target(true)
        .with_thread_ids(true)
        .with_thread_names(true)
        .with_file(true)
        .with_line_number(true)
        .with_span_events(fmt::format::FmtSpan::NEW | fmt::format::FmtSpan::CLOSE)
        .flatten_event(true)
        .with_current_span(true);

    // Sampling layer — reduce log volume
    let sampling_layer = SamplingLayer::new(0.1); // 10% of DEBUG/TRACE

    Registry::default()
        .with(env_filter)
        .with(sampling_layer)
        .with(json_layer)
        .init();
}

/// Sampling layer that passes all ERROR/WARN/INFO but samples DEBUG/TRACE.
struct SamplingLayer {
    sample_rate: f64,
}

impl SamplingLayer {
    fn new(sample_rate: f64) -> Self {
        Self { sample_rate }
    }
}

impl<S: Subscriber> Layer<S> for SamplingLayer {
    fn enabled(
        &self,
        metadata: &tracing::Metadata<'_>,
        _ctx: tracing_subscriber::layer::Context<'_, S>,
    ) -> bool {
        match *metadata.level() {
            Level::ERROR | Level::WARN | Level::INFO => true,
            Level::DEBUG | Level::TRACE => {
                // Sample based on hash of callsite for consistency
                let hash = fxhash::hash64(metadata.callsite().into());
                (hash as f64 / u64::MAX as f64) < self.sample_rate
            }
        }
    }
}

mod fxhash {
    pub fn hash64(data: impl std::hash::Hash) -> u64 {
        use std::hash::Hasher;
        let mut hasher = std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher::new();
        data.hash(&mut hasher);
        hasher.finish()
    }
}
```

```rust
// src/middleware.rs — Correlation ID middleware for axum
use axum::{
    extract::Request,
    middleware::Next,
    response::Response,
};
use tracing::{info_span, Instrument};
use uuid::Uuid;

pub async fn correlation_id_middleware(
    req: Request,
    next: Next,
) -> Response {
    // Extract or generate correlation ID
    let correlation_id = req
        .headers()
        .get("x-correlation-id")
        .and_then(|v| v.to_str().ok())
        .map(String::from)
        .unwrap_or_else(|| Uuid::new_v4().to_string());

    let method = req.method().clone();
    let uri = req.uri().path().to_string();

    // Create span with correlation ID — all child logs inherit this
    let span = info_span!(
        "http_request",
        correlation_id = %correlation_id,
        method = %method,
        path = %uri,
        status = tracing::field::Empty,
        latency_ms = tracing::field::Empty,
    );

    async move {
        let start = std::time::Instant::now();
        let mut response = next.run(req).await;

        // Record response metadata in the span
        let status = response.status().as_u16();
        let latency = start.elapsed().as_millis();
        tracing::Span::current()
            .record("status", status)
            .record("latency_ms", latency);

        // Add correlation ID to response headers
        response.headers_mut().insert(
            "x-correlation-id",
            correlation_id.parse().unwrap(),
        );

        response
    }
    .instrument(span)
    .await
}
```

```rust
// src/main.rs — Using the telemetry setup
use axum::{routing::get, Router};
use tracing::{info, error, instrument};

mod telemetry;
mod middleware;

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    telemetry::init_production_logging();

    let app = Router::new()
        .route("/users/:id", get(get_user))
        .layer(axum::middleware::from_fn(middleware::correlation_id_middleware));

    info!(port = 8080, "Server starting");
    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080").await.unwrap();
    axum::serve(listener, app).await.unwrap();
}

#[instrument(fields(user_id = %id))]
async fn get_user(axum::extract::Path(id): axum::extract::Path<String>) -> String {
    info!("Fetching user");

    match fetch_from_db(&id).await {
        Ok(user) => {
            info!(found = true, "User retrieved");
            user
        }
        Err(e) => {
            // Error with full context — correlation_id inherited from parent span
            error!(
                error = %e,
                error.source = e.source().map(|s| s.to_string()),
                "Failed to fetch user"
            );
            "error".to_string()
        }
    }
}

// Output (JSON, one line):
// {"timestamp":"2026-03-20T10:00:00Z","level":"INFO","target":"my_service",
//  "message":"User retrieved","correlation_id":"abc-123","method":"GET",
//  "path":"/users/42","user_id":"42","found":true,"thread.id":1}
```
